channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: Don’t Freak Out About Fruit-Flavored Vapes
item.description: Fruit-flavoured vapes are often treated as a youth problem, but for many adult smokers they are part of what makes switching away from cigarettes work. Flavours can help distance people from the taste and ritual of smoking while keeping them within legal, regulated markets. Protecting young people matters, but panic-driven flavour bans risk removing one of the tools adults use to quit combustible tobacco. Regulation should target youth access without undermining harm reduction. (Published on June 23, 2026bythefp.com)

item.title: MAGERO: Who will speak for the smokers struggling to quit?
item.description: Smokers struggling to quit are too often left out of tobacco policy debates. Many have tried conventional methods without success, yet lower-risk alternatives are frequently restricted, stigmatised or treated like cigarettes. Harm reduction starts by recognising smokers as people who need practical options, not moral judgement. Public health should speak for those still facing the greatest risk — and help them move away from combustible tobacco. (Published on June 23, 2026bythe-star.co.ke)

item.title: Innovation the “key” to helping Europe go “smoke free”
item.description: Tobacco policy too often speaks about smokers without listening to them. Adults who smoke understand the barriers to quitting, the limits of conventional support and the appeal of lower-risk alternatives better than any committee room. If regulation is meant to reduce harm, consumer experience should be treated as evidence, not an inconvenience. Ask the smoker — not only the room. (Published on June 23, 2026bytogethermag.eu)

item.title: The Swedish Snus Fight
item.description: Sweden’s fight over snus is really a fight over whether Europe will recognise harm reduction. Snus has helped Sweden reach some of the lowest smoking rates in the EU, yet Brussels continues to treat smoke-free nicotine products with deep suspicion. Restricting or taxing lower-risk alternatives like cigarettes risks weakening the very model that has reduced smoking-related harm. Europe should learn from Sweden’s results, not punish them. (Published on June 23, 2026byclearingtheair.eu)

item.title: CAPHRA warns Philippines health controversy raises new questions over WHO and Bloomberg policy influence
item.description: The Philippines health controversy is raising wider questions about transparency, influence and trust in tobacco policy. Harm reduction advocates warn that international pressure from the WHO and Bloomberg-linked networks may be shaping national decisions in ways that overlook local evidence, consumer needs and lower-risk alternatives. Public health policy should be accountable to the people it affects, not driven by opaque influence campaigns. Trust depends on open debate, clear evidence and regulation that reflects real-world outcomes. (Published on June 23, 2026byprlog.org)
